short: In 1974, Robyn Stewart took a job serving drinks at 30,000 feet. She never stopped flying. GOLDEN WINGS: FIFTY YEAR FLIGHT PATH traces her journey from flight attendant to commercial pilot — a half-century career that threads through the upheavals of American aviation and arrives somewhere no one told her to expect.
long:
In the early 1970s, airlines hired stewardesses for their looks and fired them when they married or turned thirty-two. Robyn Stewart took the job anyway. She had other plans.

Over the next five decades she would earn her instrument rating, then her commercial license, then her ATP. She would work regional carriers through deregulation, survive the consolidation era, and eventually occupy a cockpit seat that women of her generation were told — politely, firmly, repeatedly — was not for them.

GOLDEN WINGS: FIFTY YEAR FLIGHT PATH is built from Robyn's personal logbooks, family photographs, archival footage of the industry she inhabited, and new interviews with the people who watched her fly. It traces not only her career but the aviation lineage woven through her family — a thread that stretches back further than Robyn herself, and that her own choices extended forward.

Directed by Caleb Mills Stewart for Get Booked Studio, the film asks what it costs to love something that was never designed to love you back. And what it looks like when you stay long enough to change it.
